Interactive Visualization & Analytics

TTC Ridership and Service Dynamics

This dashboard blends all-time disruption history with a recent-period service benchmark. It uses TTC delay records from 2014 through January 2026 plus GTFS schedule structure to show where the network experiences the most friction and which high-service corridors absorb the most disruption.

Generated: 2026-03-17 16:40:32
Source: TTC CSV folders supplied for the project
Dashboard file: Self-contained HTML
Ridership note Direct ridership counts are not present in the provided CSVs. The dashboard uses GTFS scheduled trips and route stop coverage as a service-demand proxy, so “ridership-sensitive” findings should be read as exposure to passenger demand rather than exact boardings.

All-Time Disruption Trend

Monthly incident volume and accumulated delay minutes across the available history.

Analyst Readout

High-signal observations based on the selected mode.

    Coverage Snapshot

    Data horizon, recent window, and GTFS benchmark coverage.

    Service Exposure vs. Disruption Load

    Each bubble is a route. Higher vertical position means more incidents per 1,000 scheduled trips.

    Highest Pressure Locations

    Recent-period hotspots by incident count and delay minutes.

    Day and Hour Heatmap

    Delay-minute intensity across the week for the recent 2025-2026 period.

    Dominant Delay Codes

    Most frequent recent causes in the selected mode.

    Top Routes Table

    Recent routes with the largest disruption burden after GTFS benchmarking.

    The source code-description lookup tables appear to be cross-labeled across TTC folders. This dashboard remaps them by observed code overlap before displaying descriptions.
